title: Sri Muninaga Reddy vs Sri J Ashwath Rao on 27 September, 2023
body: Sri Muninaga Reddy vs Sri J Ashwath Rao on 27 September, 2023 Author: S.G.Pandit Bench: S.G.Pandit IN THE HIGH COURT OF KARNATAKA AT BENGALURU DATED THIS THE 27TH DAY OF SEPTEMBER, 2023 BEFORE THE HON'BLE MR.JUSTICE S.G.PANDIT WRIT PETITION No.12964/2022 (GM- CPC ) C/W WRIT PETITION No.21763/2021 (GM- CPC ) W.P.NO.12964/2022 BETWEEN: SRI MUNINAGA REDDY @ M N REDDY S/O LATE MUNISWAMY REDDY AGED ABOUT 70 YEARS RESIDING AT NO.45 3RD B MAIN, 4TH CROSS 3RD B BLOCK, KALYANANAGAR BENGALURU-560043. ...PETITIONER (BY SRI VARADARAJAN M S, ADV.) FOR R9-R15 V/O DATED 14.02.2023 NOTICE TO R1-R3, R5-R8, R16, R18-21 ARE D/W R4, R13, R17 ARE SERVED & UNREPRESENTED) THIS WRIT PETITION IS FILED UNDER ARTICLE 227 OF THE CONSTITUTION OF INDIA PRAYING TO QUASH THE ORDER DATED 04.11.2019 ONE PASSED BY HE XV ADDL. CITY CIVIL JUDGE BENGALURU ON IA NO.1/2013 IN O.S.NO.4879/1988 ANNEXURE-A TO THE PETITION. THESE WRIT PETITIONS HAVING BEEN HEARD AND RESERVED ON 24/08/2023 COMING ON FOR PRONOUNCEMENT THIS DAY, THE COURT MADE THE FOLLOWING: 11 ORDER In W.P.No.12964/2022, petitioner is before this Court under Article 227 of the Constitution of India aggrieved by order dated 24.02.2022 allowing I.A.No.2/2010 filed under Sections 152 and 153 of CPC permitting to implead relevant portion in the final decree in O.S.No.4879/1988 on the file of the XV Additional City Civil & Sessions Judge at Bengaluru (CCH.No.3) (for short, 'Trial Court'). 2. In W.P.No.21763/2021, petitioner is before this Court under Article 227 of the Constitution of India aggrieved by order dated 04.11.2019 in O.S.No.4879/1988 rejecting I.A.No.1/2013 filed under Order 1 Rule 10(2) of CPC to implead applicant himself as additional defendant in the suit. 3. Since the above two writ petitions arise from O.S.No.4879/1988 and as the parties and facts involved 12 are one and the same, both the writ petitions are taken up together and disposed of by this common order. 4. Brief facts of the case are that respondent Nos.1 and 2/plaintiffs filed O.S.No.4879/1988 for partition of the suit schedule properties against respondent Nos.3 to 21. The suit ended in compromise on 03.03.2004. On the compromise petition filed by the parties, Trial Court passed compromise decree on 03.03.2004. Under the compromise, plaintiffs were allotted 'E' Schedule property, whereas defendant No.3 was allotted 'C1 and C2' schedule annexed to the compromise. In terms of compromise, 'E' schedule property allotted to the respondent Nos.1 and 2/ plaintiffs herein reads as follows: "'E' SCHEDULE Land measuring 84 acres 9 guntas in extent, allotted to the share of J. Ashwath Rao and J.Subba Rao, Comprised out of portions of Sy. No. 32 measuring 6 acres 36 13 guntas (Kharab Land); Sy.No.33 measuring 5 acres 17 guntas; Sy.No.41 measuring 2 acres; Sy.No.32 measuring 2 acres 39 guntas: Sy.No.34 measuring 4 acres 9 guntas; Sy.No.35 measuring 5 acres 25 guntas; Sy.No.38 measuring 5 acres 21 guntas; Sy.No.39 measuring 5 acres 26 guntas; Sy.No.44 measuring 5 acres 4 guntas; Sy.No.43 measuring 4 acres 11 guntas: Sy.No.45 measuring 19 acres 16 guntas; Sy.No.46 measuring 5 acres 19 guntas; Sy.No.47 measuring 5 acres 24 guntas; Sy.No.48 measuring 6 acres 4 guntas; Sy.No.49 measuring 5 acres 22 guntas and Sy.No.50 measuring 1 acres 28 guntas situated at Chokkanahalli Village, Yelahanka Hobli, Bangalore Taluk with different boundaries. Sy.No.32 measuring 6 Acres and 36 Guntas: East by : Property of Narayana Reddy and Ramalinga Reddy. West by : Tirumanahalli Gadi and Property of Ramachandra Reddy 14 North by : Tirumenahalli Gadi, South by : Land bearing Sy. No. 33 Sy. Nos.33, is bounded on: East by : Property of Ramalinga Reddy West by : Sy.No.30 North by: Sy.No.32, South by: Sy.Nos.34 Sy. No.41 is bounded on: East by: Property fallen to the share Jayarama Reddy West by: Property of Prasad Reddy. North by: Property of Ramachandra Reddy. South by: Sy.No.44 Sy.No.32 measuring 2 acres 39 guntas is bounded on: East by: Kannur and Nagareshwara Naganahalli Gadi West by: Property fallen to the share of Padmamma and Kantamma North by: Kannur Gadi South by: Sy.No.42. 15 Nos. 34, 35, 38, 39, 44, 43, 45, 46, 47, 48, 49, 58 is bounded by a Common Boundary. : East by: Nagareshwara Naganahalli Gadi, West by: Property Fallen to the share of Chinnamma and Narayanareddy in Sy.No.34, 66, 52, 51, 53, 55, 56 and Nagareshwara Naganahalli Gadi. North by: Sy.No.33, and properties of Ramalinga Reddy and Narasa Reddy, Prasad Reddy and Sy.No.41 and Jayarama Reddy Padmamma's Properties. South by: Village of Nagareshwara Naganahalli Gadi." 5. The properties allotted to defendant No.3 as shown in schedule 'C1 and C2' to compromise decree reads as follows: "C1 SCHEDULE Portion of Land bearing Sy.No.32, measuring 26 acres 03 guntas in extent, allotted to the share of Narayana Reddy (D3) situated at Chokknahalli village, Yelahanka Hobli, Bangalore North Taluk and bounded on: 16 East by: Property fallen to the share of Shakuntalamma and Jayarama Reddy. West by: Property fallen to the share of J.Ashwath Rao. North by: Tirumenahalli, Bellahalli and Kannur Gadi (Border) South by: Properties fallen to the Shares of Ramalinga Reddy, C.M. Narayana Reddy, Prasad Reddy and Jayarama Reddy. SCHEDULE "C2" Portion of Land bearing Sy.No.34 measuring 1 acre 5 guntas in extent allotted to the share of Narayanareddy (D3) situated at Chokkanahalli village, Yelahanka Hobli, Bangalore North Taluk and bounded on: Easy: Remaining Portion of Sy.No.34 fallen to the share of G.Ashwath Rao West by: Portion of Sy.No.34 fallen to the share of Chinnamma North by: Road and Sy.No.33 South by: Sy.No.66" 17 6. The plaintiffs on partition having taken possession of the shares allotted to them and on getting their names entered in the revenue records, sold the entire properties under registered sale deed dated 16.08.2005 to the petitioner herein/impleading applicant in O.S.No.4879/1988. The defendant No.3 to the suit Narayana Reddy died subsequent to compromise decree on 21.09.2005. Long after the compromise decree and long after the death of defendant No.3, LRs of defendant No.3 filed application under Sections 152 and 153 of CPC on 18.12.2010 in O.S.No.4879/1988 praying to amend the compromise decree to insert the 'C1' schedule properties, as follows: "1. (a) in the 1st and 2nd line of C1 schedule property at page 13, to insert Sy.No.32 measuring 23 acres 03 guntas along with 6 acre 36 guntas of phot Kharab (b) in the boundaries, bunded on west by: "thimmenahalli Gadi" instead of property fallen to the share of 'J' schedule holder i.e., 18 Ashwath Rao. Accordingly, in view of this amendment sought, kindly amend the judgment and Final decree as follows:- 2. In the final decree portion of defendant 3 In Schedule 'C1' property at page 6, in line 1 and 2, to amend as follows: "along with 6 A, 36 G of phot Kharab" and in line 8, "Thimmenahalli Gadi" instead of property fallen to the share of J.Ashwath Rao, in the interest of justice and equity." 7. In the affidavit annexed to the said application filed under Sections 152 and 153 of CPC, LRs of defendant No.3 stated that plaintiffs/respondent Nos.1 and 2 herein got totally an extent of 84 acres 13 guntas and defendant No.3 - Narayana Reddy got totally an extent of 27 acres 08 guntas. It is also stated that a sketch was also attached to the compromise petition which is part and parcel of the compromise 19 petition. It is the case of the LRs of defendant No.3 that phot Kharab to an extent of 6 acres 36 guntas in Sy.No.32 is adjacent to the land allotted to their share and in pursuance to compromise decree, entry was made in the record of rights including 6 acres 36 guntas phot Kharab in the name of defendant No.3 and he was in possession. It is stated that 6 acres 36 guntas which is part and parcel of the land fallen to the share of their father defendant No.3 in Sy.No.32, but inadvertently it was mentioned in the text of the schedule of compromise petition that phot Kharab had fallen to the share of plaintiffs/respondent Nos.1 and 2 herein under Schedule-E to the compromise. It is also stated in the affidavit that purchaser, petitioner herein made application for conversion of phot Kharab land before the Deputy Commissioner, Bengaluru North and the Deputy Commissioner passed an order dated 02.12.2009 in favour of Sri.Muninaga Reddy. Challenging the said 20 order, LRs of defendant No.3 filed W.P.No.32735/2010 before this Court and this Court by order dated 02.12.2010 rejected the said writ petition with an observation to get the compromise decree corrected. As such, it is stated that LRs of defendant No.3 filed application under Sections 152 and 153 of CPC to amend the compromise decree. 8. The Trial Court allowed I.A filed under Sections 152 and 153 of CPC observing that phot Kharab when it is part and parcel of the particular survey number, it cannot be separately sold by the plaintiff and even if the plaintiff sold the same, it will not create any right in favour of the purchaser. It is also observed that while preparing sketch there is mistake crept in. The trial Court proceeded to allow the application on the premise that Sections 152 and 153 of CPC empowers correction of clerical or arithmetical mistakes in judgments, decrees or orders or errors arising therein 21 from any accidental slip or omission and that Court could permit amend of any defect or error in any proceedings in a suit and all necessary amendment shall be made for the purpose of determining the real question or issue raised by or depending on such proceedings. Challenging the said order, petitioner/impleading applicant is before this Court in this writ petition. 9. Further, petitioner/impleading applicant is also before this Court challenging order rejecting his application filed under Order I Rule 10(2) of CPC to get himself impleaded as additional defendant. 10. Learned counsel for the petitioner Sri.Varadarajan.M.S., would contend that respondent Nos.1 and 2/plaintiffs in pursuance to the compromise decree dated 03.03.2004 conveyed the entire 'E' schedule property to the petitioner under registered sale deed dated 22 16.08.2005 for valuable consideration. Since the property is sold by respondent Nos.1 and 2 in favour of petitioner herein, respondent Nos.1 and 2 have no interest in the property in question. Since the petitioner has entered the shoes of respondent Nos.1 and 2 having purchased the property fallen to the share of respondent Nos.1 and 2/plaintiffs, petitioner is an interested person. In the absence of respondent Nos.1 and 2/plaintiffs, to contest the application for correction or amendment of decree, the petitioner to protect his interest, opposed the application and he is before this Court challenging the order passed on I.A.No.2/2010 allowing amendment of decree. 11. Learned counsel for the petitioner would further submit that the compromise decree passed in O.S.No.4879/1988 has become final and the Court has no power to amend or modify such decree in exercise of its power under Sections 152 or 153 of CPC . The Trial Court failed to appreciate that compromise .decree could 23 not have been amended unless parties to the compromise consent for such amendment. Moreover, he submits that when third party interest has been created in pursuance to compromise decree dated 03.03.2004, Trial Court ought not to have entertained application to amend the compromise decree. Learned counsel would further contend that Trial Court could not have passed order affecting third party rights, when specifically it was brought to the notice of the Trial Court that in pursuance to the compromise decree, respondent Nos.1 and 2/plaintiffs have sold the property under registered sale deed dated 16.08.2005 in favour of petitioner. 12. Learned counsel for the petitioner would submit that in exercise of its power under Sections 152 or 153 of CPC , the Court could correct the clerical or arithmetical error crept in, in the decree. In exercise of its correctional jurisdiction, the Trial Court could not have amended the decree itself. Learned counsel would further 24 submit that Court has power to rectify defects or mistakes or errors in compromise decree so as to bring the decree in conformity with compromise petition. Learned counsel would point out that it is not the case of the respondents - LRs of defendant No.3 that compromise decree is contrary to the compromise petition. It is the case of the respondents that by mistake, Kharab to an extent of 6 acres 36 guntas in Sy.No.32 is included in the schedule allotted to the share of respondent Nos.1 and 2/plaintiffs. Learned counsel would submit that it is not the mistake of the Court which has crept in, in the compromise decree to exercise its power under Sections 152 and 153 of CPC for correction. It is submitted that Court under compromise decree accepted the compromise petition filed by the parties to the compromise as it is. When there is no allegation of fraud or misrepresentation or misunderstanding between the parties, the Court could not have exercised its power to amend the compromise 25 decree. It is the case of the petitioner that inadvertently, Kharab of 6 acres 36 guntas in Sy.No.32 is included in the schedule of lands allotted to the share of respondent Nos.1 and 2/plaintiffs. Further, learned counsel would submit that defendant No.3 during his lifetime has not come forward with such a contention that by inadvertently or mistakenly Kharab of 6 acres 36 guntas in Sy.No.32 is included in the schedule allotted to the plaintiffs i.e., respondent Nos.1 and 2 herein. Learned counsel for the petitioner places reliance on the decision of the Hon'ble Apex Court in the case of STATE OF PUNJAB VS. DARSHAN SINGH1 to contend that power to correct clerical or arithmetical errors in judgment under Section 152 of CPC cannot be equated with power of review. The omission or mistake which goes to the merits of the case cannot be corrected. Learned counsel would further submit that power of correction under Sections 152 and 1 2003 AIR SCW 5488 26 153 of CPC cannot be exercised when the same is not vitiated by fraud, misrepresentation or patent or obvious mistake. In that regard, he places reliance on AJANTA LLP VS. CASIO KEISANKI KABUSHIKI KAISHA D/B/A CASIO COMPUTER COMPANY LIMITED AND ANOTHER2 . 13. Per contra, learned counsel Sri.Vinod Prasad for respondents - LRs of defendant No.3 would submit that suit for partition among parties in O.S.No.4879/1988 was compromised and compromise decree was drawn in terms of compromise petition dated 03.03.2004. Learned counsel would submit that by mistake or inadvertently, Kharab which is adjacent to the lands allotted to the share of defendant No.3 was shown in the schedule 'E' lands allotted to the plaintiffs. To correct such mistake inadvertently taken place, LRs of defendant No.3 filed I.A.No.2/2010 under Sections 152 and 153 of CPC to correct the compromise decree. 2 (2022) 5 SCC 449 27 Learned counsel would submit that Trial Court rightly allowed the said application and under impugned order directed correction of compromise decree permitting the LRs of defendant No.3 to include and to amend the decree as prayed in I.A.No.2/2010. Learned counsel would submit that the decree is amended to give effect to the true and real compromise between the parties. Moreover, he submits that land in question i.e., 6 acres 36 guntas in Sy.No.32 (phot Kharab) is in possession and enjoyment of LRs of defendant No.3. Therefore, learned counsel would justify the order passed by Trial Court. Learned counsel would submit that even in the absence of allegation of fraud or misrepresentation, the Court could exercise its power under Sections 152 and 153 of CPC to correct the error which has occurred in the compromise decree. Learned counsel would submit that Trial Court is empowered under Sections 151 of CPC 28 to entertain such application questioning the legality or validity of compromise decree. 14. On hearing the learned counsel appearing for the parties and on perusal of the writ petition papers, the only point which falls for consideration is as to, "In the facts of the present case, whether the Trial Court is justified in exercising its power under Sections 152 and 153 of CPC to amend the consent decree?" The answer to the above point would be that Trial Court is not justified in exercising its power under Sections 152 and 153 of CPC to amend the consent decree, for the following reasons: 15. The respondent Nos.1 and 2/plaintiffs filed O.S.No.4879/1988 for partition and separate possession of the suit schedule properties. The suit was compromised and consent decree was passed on 03.03.2004. Under the compromise, respondent Nos.1 29 and 2/plaintiffs were allotted share in terms of 'E' schedule i.e., plaintiffs were allotted totally 84 acres 09 guntas in various survey numbers along with 6 acres 36 guntas of Kharab land in Sy.No.32. The defendant No.3 was allotted 'C1' and 'C2' schedules. It is not the case of the petitioners/LRs of defendant No.3 that compromise decree dated 03.03.2004 is not in consonance with or is not in terms of the compromise petition. The compromise decree dated 03.03.2004 is drawn strictly in terms and in consonance with compromise petition filed by the parties to the suit. It is the case of the petitioners/LRs of defendant No.3 that, by inadvertently or mistakenly, Kharab land measuring 6 acres 36 guntas in Sy.No.32 which is adjacent to the land allotted to their share is included in the schedule allotted to the respondent Nos.1 and 2/plaintiffs. In other words, it is the case of the petitioners/LRs of defendant No.3 that there is a mistake in the compromise petition itself. If 30 the compromise petition itself suffers from any mistake by inadvertently or otherwise which according to the parties require rectification, the remedy is not an application under Sections 152 or 153 of CPC to correct the compromise or consent decree. 16. Sections 152 and 153 of CPC reads as follows: "152. Amendment of judgments, decrees or orders.-- Clerical or arithmetical mistakes in judgments, decrees or orders or errors arising therein from any accidental slip or omission may at any time be corrected by the Court either of its own motion or on the application of any of the parties. 153. General power to amend.-- The Court may at any time, and on such terms as to costs or otherwise as it may think fit, amend any defect or error in any proceeding in a suit; and all necessary amendments shall be made for the purpose of determining the real question or issue raised by or depending on such proceeding." 31 17. A reading of the above provisions, it is abundantly clear that the Court is empowered to order amendment of judgments, decrees or orders if it finds clerical or arithmetical mistakes in judgments, decrees or orders or errors arising from any accidental slip or omission. Such correction can be either on application by either of the parties or on its own motion. The power conferred under Sections 152 and 153 of CPC cannot be exercised to amend the compromise petition itself. 18. Section 151 of CPC can be invoked if the compromise was vitiated by fraud, misrepresentation or mistake and Court could rectify a consent decree. But, in the instant case, it is not the contention of the petitioners/LRs of defendant No.3 that consent decree or compromise vitiates by fraud, misrepresentation or mistake. 19. The Hon'ble Apex Court in AJANTA LLP (supra) was considering an appeal arising from 32 judgment dismissing application filed under Sections 152 and 153 of CPC read with Section 151 of CPC seeking modification of the judgment passed accepting application filed under Order XXIII Rule 3 of CPC . The Hon'ble Apex Court at paragraphs 22 and 24 has observed as follows: "22. A consent decree would not serve as an estoppel, where the compromise was vitiated by fraud, misrepresentation, or mistake. The Court in exercise of its inherent power may rectify the consent decree to ensure that it is free from clerical or arithmetical errors so as to bring it in conformity with the terms of the compromise. Undoubtedly, the Court can entertain an application under Section 151 CPC for alterations/modification of the consent decree if the same is vitiated by fraud, misrepresentation, or misunderstanding. 24. The High Court applied its mind and passed a decree in terms of the settlement agreement dated 16-5-2019. Though, the High Court dismissed the application by 33 refusing to entertain the application on the ground that it was filed under Section 152 CPC, we have considered the submissions of the parties to examine whether the appellant has made out a case for modification of the decree by treating the application as one under the proviso to Order 23 Rule 3 read with Section 151 CPC. There is no allegation either of fraud or misrepresentation on the part of the respondent. We are unable to agree with the appellant that there was a mistake committed while entering into a settlement agreement due to misunderstanding. Correspondence between the advocates for the parties who are experts in law would show that there is no ambiguity or lack of clarity giving rise to any misunderstanding. Even assuming there is a mistake, a consent decree cannot be modified/altered unless the mistake is a patent or obvious mistake. Or else, there is a danger of every consent decree being sought to be altered on the ground of 34 mistake/misunderstanding by a party to the consent decree." 20. The Hon'ble Apex Court in the above decision has made it clear that a consent decree cannot be modified or altered unless the mistake is patent or obvious mistake. Further, it has also observed that a consent decree cannot be modified/altered unless the mistake is a patent or obvious mistake, or else there is a danger of every consent decree being sought to be altered on the ground of mistake/misunderstanding by a party to the consent decree. 21. The Hon'ble Apex Court in DARSHAN SINGH (supra), has made it clear that Section 152 provides for correction of clerical or arithmetical mistake in judgments, decrees or orders or errors arising from any accidental slip or omission. Further, it observed that powers under Section 152 of the Code are neither to be equated with the power of review or even said to clothe 35 the Court concerned under the guise of invoking after the result of the judgment earlier rendered, in its entirety or any portion or part of it. It is also made clear that omission sought to be corrected which goes to the merits of the case is beyond the scope of Section 152 . It is observed that no Court can, under the cover of Sections 151 and 152 of CPC modify, alter or add to the terms of its original judgment, decree or order. 22. A co-ordinate Bench of this Court in the case of M.K.PURUSHOTTAM VS. SMT.MALLAMMA AND OTHERS3 was considering similar fact situation. The facts of the said case are that the Trial Court passed a Common judgment. Thereafter applications were filed under Sections 151 and 152 of CPC seeking to amend the compromise decree as well as compromise petition to the effect that they intend to correct the area of land in question which was 3 ILR 2000 KAR 3549 36 mentioned in the compromise decree as well as in the compromise petition. In that circumstances, this Court has observed that scope of Section 152 of CPC is only to correct the clerical or arithmetical mistake or to make clerical correction in the compromise decree, if the decree which has been drawn is not in consonance and in conformity with the terms of the compromise. 23. In the case on hand, the parties to the suit entered into compromise and field compromise petition settling their dispute. In terms of the compromise petition, Trial Court passed consent or compromise decree. The Trial Court accepted what is filed before the Court by the parties. The compromise or consent decree was in conformity or in consonance with the compromise petition filed by the parties. If there is inadvertent mistake in the compromise petition itself, it is not the mistake of the Court. When the Court has accepted compromise petition filed by the parties as it is, the Court could not have 37 exercised its power under Sections 152 and 153 of CPC to amend the compromise decree. When there is no allegation of fraud or misrepresentation or misunderstanding between the parties, the Trial Court could not have exercised its power to amend the compromise decree. Moreover, as stated above, in the case on hand to exercise power under Sections 152 and 153 of CPC, it is not the mistake of the Court. In other words, if clerical or arithmetical mistakes or error or omission is by Court, then it could exercise jurisdiction under Sections 152 and 153 of CPC. The Trial Court exceeded its jurisdiction while exercising power under Sections 152 and 153 of CPC, which has resulted in miscarriage of justice. 24. It is also pertinent to note that LRs of defendant No.3 filed application under Sections 152 and 153 of CPC nearly after 7 years from the date of compromise decree. Admittedly, defendant No.3 during 38 his lifetime has not filed application for correction of decree or correction of compromise petition. If the parties have entered in the compromise with full knowledge and if the intention of the parties at the time of entering into compromise to allot 6 acres 36 guntas of Kharab land in Sy.No.32 to respondent Nos.1 and 2/plaintiffs, LRs of defendant No.3 at this stage, that too, when third party rights have been created under registered sale deed dated 16.08.2005 in favour of petitioner herein, could not have filed such application, that too in the absence of petitioners herein. The petitioner herein on purchase from respondent Nos.1 and 2/plaintiffs property allotted to them, under registered sale deed dated 16.08.2005, acquired right, title and interest over 'E' schedule of the compromise petition and they have entered the shoes of respondent Nos.1 and 2/plaintiffs. 25. For the reasons recorded above, I proceed to pass the following: 39 ORDER 1) W.P.No.12964/2022 is allowed. 2) Order dated 24.02.2022 in O.S.No.4879/1988 on the file of the XV Additional City Civil and Sessions Judge at Bengaluru (CCH-3) allowing I.A.No.2/2010 filed under Sections 152 and 153 of CPC is set aside. 3) Consequently, order dated 04.11.2019 in O.S.No.4879/1988 rejecting I.A.No.1/2013 filed under Order 1 Rule 10(2) of CPC by petitioner would no more survive for consideration. Accordingly, W.P.No.21763/2021 stands disposed of. Sd/- JUDGE NC. CT:bms
